pair_convention.spot_explanation: AUD/CAD at 0.9510 means 1 AUD buys 0.9510 CAD. A rising AUD/CAD rate indicates AUD strengthening versus CAD (it takes more CAD to buy one AUD). A falling AUD/CAD rate indicates CAD strengthening versus AUD (it takes fewer CAD to buy one AUD). The base currency is AUD and the quote currency is CAD.

executive_summary: AUD/CAD trades at 0.9510 as of March 11, 2026, up approximately 6.4% year-on-year and roughly 2.8% off the July 2025 trough near 0.8880. The pair currently sits approximately 4.6% above OECD PPP fair value of 0.91, with spot well above all major moving averages — the pair has re-rated sharply since the RBA's surprise February hike to 3.85%, which generated a 160-basis-point policy rate advantage over the BoC's 2.25%. The dominant bilateral narrative is one of reinforcing policy divergence: Australia is the sole G10 central bank actively hiking while Canada completed a 275bp easing cycle and now sits at the lower bound of its own neutral range. This rate gap is the widest since the RBA was last the G10 hawkish outlier in 2022–2023 and is the primary structural driver of the pair's re-rating. Tactically, however, the setup is complicated by extreme long-AUD positioning at the 100th percentile COT Index, which creates asymmetric unwind risk; the March 17–18 RBA-FOMC double event; and a pair that is approaching significant technical resistance near 0.9645–0.9700. We are moderately bullish AUD/CAD with tactical conviction 2/5 and strategic conviction 3/5, viewing the current 0.9510 level as expensive tactically given positioning crowding but justified directionally by the policy and growth differential — the preferred entry is a pullback toward 0.9350–0.9420 to capture the strategic carry and rate divergence trade at a more favourable risk/reward.

market_context.valuation_assessment: AUD/CAD trades at approximately 5–6% premium to OECD PPP fair value of approximately 0.91 (AUD dossier shows +4.6% above PPP-implied fair value). The pair has traversed from deep value at the July 2025 trough of 0.8840 through fair value at approximately 0.91 and is now modestly above PPP, meaning valuation has shifted from a tailwind to a mild headwind. The rate premium of +160 bps partially justifies the deviation from PPP, but the structural carry advantage shrinks as the pair moves further above PPP. Bilateral fair value is anchored at approximately 0.91–0.95 based on both dossiers.

market_context.recent_regime: AUD/CAD's regime over the past 2–4 weeks is best characterised as 'extended rally approaching resistance exhaustion.' The pair rallied from approximately 0.88 in late 2025 to a 2026 high near 0.9645–0.9700 in mid-February as the RBA hike re-priced the bilateral carry advantage. Since then, the pair has pulled back toward 0.9500–0.9560, with the retreat driven by a combination of Iran-related risk-off (which hurts AUD more than CAD given CAD's oil tailwind), overbought momentum readings, and resistance at multi-year highs. Patience may be needed on AUD/CAD, with both the RBA and BoC likely to deliver hawkish holds, keeping both currencies vulnerable to bouts of strength and leaving the cross confined to its broader range while key levels remain intact.

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The RBA-BoC bilateral carry of +160 bps is the centrepiece of the AUD/CAD thesis and the widest RBA-BoC differential since 2013. The 10-year sovereign spread (AU ACGB 4.97% vs CGB 3.42% = +155 bps) is consistent with the policy rate differential at the long end. The carry-to-vol ratio of approximately 0.19 is modest but meaningful — carry covers approximately one-fifth of annual vol, comparable to the AUD/JPY carry-to-vol ratio before it became a consensus trade. Carry is positively contributing but not yet dominant; with the pair 5–6% above PPP fair value, carry is working to maintain the level premium but not driving further re-rating on its own. Forward points of approximately +13/+40/+80 pips at 1M/3M/6M mean buyers of AUD/CAD long accrues approximately 38 pips per quarter in carry, requiring the pair to fall by less than that amount for the carry holder to break even. The bilateral carry advantage is expected to widen from 160 bps to 185–210 bps over the strategic horizon as the RBA hikes to 4.10–4.35% while the BoC holds at 2.25%.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The RBA-BoC rate differential of +160 bps is the single most important bilateral factor for AUD/CAD. The RBA at 3.85% is the only G10 central bank actively hiking; the BoC at 2.25% is at the lower bound of its own 2.25–3.25% neutral range, having completed 275 bps of cuts. This is not merely a static spread — the trajectory is diverging: OIS markets price a 38% probability of a further RBA hike in March or May 2026, while BoC OIS prices 94% hold probability with essentially no easing through year-end. The AU-CA 10-year sovereign spread at +155 bps is consistent with the policy differential and has been a reliable leading indicator for the pair. Tactically, the 17 March RBA meeting and 18 March FOMC back-to-back create the risk of a bilateral repricing: if the RBA hikes to 4.10% and FOMC is dovish, the bilateral carry advantage jumps to +185 bps — a 25 bps widening that would mechanically drive AUD/CAD higher through the carry channel. Conversely, a dovish RBA hold paired with a hawkish FOMC (which indirectly weakens CAD less than AUD) would compress the advantage. The BoC meeting on 18 March is expected to hold with two-way guidance maintained, adding minimal new information.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic rate differential trajectory strongly favours AUD/CAD appreciation. The RBA SoMP implies a terminal rate near 4.35–4.45%, while BoC consensus sits at 2.25% through 2026 (with Scotiabank the outlier at 2.75%). Even under the base case (RBA to 4.10–4.20%, BoC holds at 2.25%), the differential widens from 160 bps to 185–195 bps over the strategic horizon — a meaningful further expansion. The Fed's expected cut to 3.25–3.50% by September does not directly change the RBA-BoC spread, but it reinforces a global environment where central bank divergence is pronounced. The CGB-ACGB 10Y spread at -155 bps (Australia higher) is likely to widen modestly as Australia's inflation persistence drives the RBA to keep hiking while Canada's weaker growth (1.1% GDP forecast for 2026) keeps the BoC on hold. This is the most important bilateral factor by weight and trajectory, providing a structural foundation for AUD/CAD targeting 0.97–1.00+ at the strategic horizon if the RBA hiking cycle completes above 4.20%.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral growth and inflation differentials are unambiguously AUD/CAD positive at the tactical horizon. Australia: GDP +2.6% YoY, trimmed mean CPI at 3.4% (16-month high), unemployment at 4.1% — unambiguously hawkish data that validates the RBA's February hike. Canada: Q4 2025 GDP contracted -0.6% annualised (the only G10 contraction), January GDP flash was flat (0.0%), and the services PMI is at 46.5 (four consecutive months of contraction). This is the starkest bilateral growth divergence within the anglophone currency group — Australia growing at 2.6% while Canada contracts. The unemployment differential reinforces the picture: Australia at 4.1% (tight, below RBA SoMP forecasts) vs Canada at 6.5% (elevated, consistent with labour market slack). The inflation differential is directionally consistent: Australian trimmed mean at 3.4% demands continued tightening; Canadian CPI-trim at 2.4% is near target, removing any urgency for the BoC to tighten. This factor is a clean bilateral bullish AUD/CAD signal.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic growth differential is bullish AUD/CAD but with narrowing conviction. Australia is projected to decelerate from 2.6% toward 1.8–2.1% GDP by December 2026 (RBA SoMP), while Canada is forecast to recover from its contraction toward 1.5% in 2027. The growth gap narrows over the strategic horizon — from approximately 1.5 ppts (Australia 2.6% vs Canada 1.1%) toward perhaps 0.5–0.8 ppts by year-end — which is still positive for AUD/CAD but less starkly so than at the tactical horizon. The unemployment gap is expected to persist (Australia ~4.3% vs Canada ~6.5%+ through mid-2026), maintaining the relative labour market quality differential. The strategic inflation picture is also moderating: the RBA SoMP projects trimmed mean CPI declining from 3.7% peak toward 2.8% by mid-2027, while Canadian CPI may briefly spike (February oil effect) before settling near 2.4%. The strategic risk to this factor is that Canadian growth recovers faster than expected (energy sector, TMX capacity), while Australian growth decelerates faster (rate hike impact on housing), narrowing the gap toward neutrality.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The risk channel for AUD/CAD is muted relative to pairs where one leg is a safe haven. Both AUD and CAD are commodity currencies with positive risk betas, so global risk-off moves tend to hit both currencies simultaneously. Given both currencies' sensitivity to industrial commodity prices, they generally move in tandem with broader risk sentiment. The AUD tends to react more to moves in industrial metals — especially copper — while the CAD is tied to oil price fluctuations. The tactical bearish stance reflects that in the current elevated-VIX environment (22.8, spiking 35+ on the Iran conflict), AUD tends to underperform CAD because: (1) AUD has a higher global risk beta (~0.7 to S&P 500 vs CAD's ~0.3–0.4) and (2) oil above $100/bbl directly benefits CAD through the terms-of-trade channel while AUD's commodity basket (iron ore, coal) is less directly affected by the Iran shock. AUD/CAD is positioned at extended premium levels following a late-stage uptrend, while macro conditions increasingly favor risk-off behavior. The Australian dollar remains vulnerable to global uncertainty and softer growth expectations, whereas the Canadian dollar is relatively supported by commodities and energy dynamics.

The pair often trades within well-defined ranges on the weekly chart rather than trending aggressively. The strategic neutrality reflects the expectation that global risk conditions normalise over 1–6 months, leaving the rate and growth differentials as the dominant pair drivers. The TSX's outperformance (+38.1% 1Y) creates a potential mean-reversion risk for Canadian equities that could weigh on CAD, partially offsetting the oil benefit.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral positioning setup is the most important tactical risk factor for AUD/CAD. The pair is essentially a double-long-AUD/double-short-CAD position in CFTC data: leveraged funds are simultaneously net long AUD (+54,635) and net short CAD (-41,371). This creates approximately $9.6 billion in notional bilateral positioning on the long-AUD/short-CAD trade — the pair trade itself is crowded. Historically, when leveraged funds are simultaneously at/near their maximum bullish extreme in one currency AND meaningfully short the other, the risk of a positioning-driven reversal is amplified. The AUD crowding (100th percentile COT Index) is the more extreme reading; the CAD shorts at ~70th–75th percentile are meaningful but not at historical extremes. The near-term reversal risk is asymmetric: incremental AUD buying power is exhausted at the 100th percentile, while CAD short-covering could add +100–150 pips if super-week catalysts are CAD-positive. The March 17–18 event cluster is the trigger: a dovish FOMC (more cuts) paired with BoC holding steady compresses the CAD-USD spread, potentially triggering short-covering in CAD that coincidentally reduces AUD/CAD demand.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over the strategic horizon, positioning extremes mean-revert as participants adjust to fundamentals. The AUD CFTC 100th percentile reading will normalise within 4–8 weeks through either a tactical pullback or time decay as new shorts are added and old longs are reduced. The CAD leveraged fund shorts (~41K) are motivated by the carry trade against USD (+138 bps) and are likely to persist as long as the Fed-BoC spread remains wide — but will reduce as the Fed cuts. The sustainable strategic positioning for AUD/CAD is probably AUD at the 65th–75th percentile (long) and CAD at flat to modestly short — a net pair position of perhaps $5–7 billion in the same direction as now, but from a less crowded starting point. The bilateral flow picture is complicated by the newly announced MOU between Canadian pension funds and Australian superannuation funds, signed March 5, 2026, to deepen investment cooperation. In 2024, Australia was the largest destination in the Indo-Pacific for Canadian direct investment, totalling $58.8 billion, while Australia's direct investment into Canada reached $27 billion. This structural bilateral investment relationship creates non-CFTC flows: Canadian pension capital flowing to Australia is AUD-positive (buying AUD assets requires selling CAD), while the MOU to facilitate Australian super fund investment in Canada could modestly increase CAD-buying demand from Australian super funds over time.

Without that confirmation, the asymmetric risk is that both legs of the crowded pair trade (long AUD + short CAD) unwind simultaneously, producing an outsized and rapid correction toward 0.9170–0.9270.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The net commodity picture is roughly neutral at the tactical horizon with opposing forces of similar magnitude: oil elevated and strongly positive for CAD while iron ore/coal/copper are elevated and positive for AUD. The Iran-driven oil premium (WTI above $80, with $100+ peak) is the dominant near-term commodity factor, and it benefits CAD disproportionately given Canada's direct oil export exposure through TMX. Australia's commodity exposure is more diversified: iron ore at $102–106/t is positive but stable (no new catalyst), while copper at $12,675/t near records is AUD-positive but only moderately weighted. The external balance comparison is notable: Australia's current account at -2.9% of GDP is widening structurally (on a deteriorating trajectory), while Canada's current account at -1.0% of GDP is more stable and likely improving as energy export revenues increase with TMX capacity.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic external balance story favours AUD/CAD from two angles. First, oil normalisation: the EIA and consensus expect WTI to retreat toward $60–70 as Iran resolves and OPEC+ unwinds. This removes the primary commodity headwind for AUD/CAD — the oil-driven CAD tailwind fades. Second, Australia's coal, copper, and LNG exposure remains elevated: thermal coal at $133–137/t is structural (energy security premium from Iran), copper near records is driven by electrification/AI data centre demand (non-cyclical), and LNG Australia has structural advantages with Gladstone/Wheatstone/Darwin facilities. However, the structural iron ore headwind (Simandou supply ramp, China property sector decline) is a genuine negative for AUD that limits the upside from the commodity channel. Canada's structural improvement from TMX (permanent WCS-WTI compression of ~$3–5/bbl) and LNG Canada (14 MTPA) is a lasting positive for CAD that partially offsets the oil normalisation scenario. The net strategic assessment is modestly bullish AUD/CAD because: oil normalisation reduces CAD's oil advantage more than it reduces AUD's diversified commodity basket; and Australia's higher-quality commodity mix (copper, coal, LNG) faces less structural headwind than Canada's primary oil dependence, where the OPEC+ oversupply equivalent limits long-term price support.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral guidance divergence is pronounced and clearly favours AUD. The RBA's communication since the February hike has been unambiguously hawkish: Governor Bullock stated 'every meeting is live,' the post-meeting statement cited 'material' inflation pickup, and the February minutes confirmed financial conditions 'may no longer be restrictive.' This is the language of a central bank that is actively tightening. The BoC, by contrast, pivoted from an implicit easing bias to explicit two-way guidance at the January 28 meeting: Governor Macklem described the rate as 'at the lower end of the neutral range and appropriate to provide some support' — language that acknowledges accommodation rather than restriction. This bilateral guidance contrast — one central bank describing conditions as 'no longer restrictive' and another saying it is providing 'support' — is as clear a divergence signal as exists in G10. The 17 March RBA meeting is more communications-important than the 18 March BoC meeting (no MPR at the BoC; the BoC holds with minimal language change expected); the asymmetry favours AUD/CAD as RBA communication is expected to provide more new information than BoC.

tactical.rationale: The tactical AUD/CAD picture is structurally bullish but tactically hazardous — a classic 'right thesis, wrong entry point' configuration. The rate differential at +160 bps is the dominant weighted contributor (+0.194), reflecting the unique bilateral dynamic where the only G10 central bank hiking (RBA) is paired against the only major central bank at the lower bound of its neutral range (BoC). The inflation and growth data amplifies this (+0.126): Australia's 2.6% GDP and 3.4% trimmed mean CPI versus Canada's Q4 contraction (-0.6% annualised) and 2.4% CPI-trim is the starkest bilateral macro divergence within G10. These two factors alone justify a materially higher AUD/CAD, and they carry a combined weight of 0.45 in the tactical calculation. However, three tactical headwinds conspire to suppress conviction below 3/5. First, CFTC positioning is at dual extremes: AUD longs at 100th percentile and CAD shorts at 70th–75th percentile create a combined $9.6 billion in leveraged positioning on the long-AUD/short-CAD trade — the marginal buyer is absent and the unwind potential is significant (-0.068 contribution). Second, the pair is approaching 5-year technical resistance at 0.9645 with momentum exhaustion signals (long upper wicks, RSI at 55–60 after being overbought at 70+), creating an adverse technical entry (-0.033). Third, the March 17–18 central bank double-event introduces binary risk: a dovish RBA hold would simultaneously justify reducing AUD longs and removing the primary carry justification for the pair trade (-0.031). The path of least resistance over the 2–4 week tactical window is a consolidation or modest pullback to the 0.9350–0.9440 zone — where the 50-day SMA and prior breakout levels sit — before the fundamental carry story can re-engage at a more attractive risk/reward. The risk/reward at current entry is mildly negative: the expected value calculation returns approximately -16.25 pips (slightly negative EV at current entry). Waiting for a pullback to 0.9350–0.9420 materially improves EV by reversing this asymmetry. The benign USD liquidity environment (+0.066) and guidance divergence (+0.050) provide constructive support but are insufficient to overcome the positioning and technical headwinds at this specific entry level.

First, the policy rate differential of +160 bps is the heaviest-weighted factor (+0.203 contribution) and is likely to widen further to +185–210 bps over the strategic horizon as the RBA hikes to 4.10–4.35% while the BoC holds at 2.25%. This represents a structural carry advantage that has historically driven AUD/CAD to 0.97–1.02 in previous episodes of comparable magnitude (2013, 2022). Second, the external balance factor carries an elevated strategic weight (0.19) given this pair's commodity-currency-vs-commodity-currency nature — and the strategic commodity net assessment is modestly bullish AUD/CAD: oil is expected to normalise from the current geopolitical premium toward $65–70 (removing CAD's primary commodity advantage), while Australia's diversified export basket (iron ore, coal, copper, LNG) faces a more gradual decline trajectory (+0.114 contribution). Third, central bank guidance (+0.078) reflects the 25 bps gap between RBA market pricing (~4.20%) and SoMP-implied terminal (~4.45%) — this repricing accrues gradually and represents the clearest quantifiable 'edge' in the pair. The bilateral investment relationship adds a structural flow dimension that most models ignore: PM Carney's MOU to deepen investment cooperation between Canadian pension funds and Australian superannuation funds (March 5, 2026) creates new bilateral cross-border flows, while IFM announced their intention to invest up to $10 billion in Canada — representing Australian super fund capital flowing into Canadian assets (AUD-selling, CAD-buying), which is a modest but structural CAD-positive flow that partially offsets the carry advantage. The strategic target is AUD/CAD 0.9700–1.0000 by mid-2026, contingent on the RBA hiking cycle completing above 4.10% and oil normalising below $75. Conviction is 3/5 rather than 4/5 because: (1) AUD's current account deficit at -2.9% of GDP (widening to potentially -3.5%) creates a structural cap on appreciation pace; (2) Canada's energy infrastructure improvements (TMX, LNG Canada) provide a sustained floor under CAD; and (3) the China risk channel affects AUD disproportionately. The benign USD liquidity environment (+0.066) and dollar weakness (DXY -9% YoY) provide additional tailwinds as the Fed cutting cycle reduces the incentive for leveraged fund CAD shorts.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: Among AUD/commodity crosses, AUD/CAD ranks 3rd most attractive long strategically: (1) Long AUD/NZD — tightest carry expression; NZ current account -5.7% vs AU -2.9%; cleanest; (2) Long AUD/EUR — RBA-ECB divergence widest in G10; structural downtrend in EUR/AUD; (3) Long AUD/CAD — +160–185 bps carry; oil normalisation removes primary CAD tailwind; commodity diversification mix net AUD positive; (4) Long AUD/JPY — highest absolute carry (+310 bps) but BoJ hiking risk and extreme PPP overvaluation (+23.8%) create largest unwind risk; (5) AUD/CHF — excellent carry-to-vol but CHF defensive characteristics limit strategic upside.

base_case.path: Phase 1 (Weeks 1–4): Consolidation and positioning reset as the March 17–18 events resolve; AUD/CAD trades 0.9350–0.9600. Phase 2 (Months 2–3): Rate differential reasserts as RBA guidance confirms May hike trajectory; oil begins normalising; pair grinds from 0.9500 toward 0.9650, testing and eventually breaking 0.9645 resistance. Phase 3 (Months 4–6): If 0.9700 confirms as new floor, CTA and momentum buying adds flows toward 0.9850–1.00.

Oil falls to $65–70 removing CAD oil premium entirely; China stimulus restores iron ore to $110+/t

strategic.path_expectation: The strategic path follows a three-phase pattern. Phase 1 (Weeks 1–4): Consolidation and positioning reset as the March 17–18 events resolve and CFTC positions normalise from extreme levels. AUD/CAD expected to trade 0.9350–0.9600. Phase 2 (Months 2–3): Rate differential reasserts as RBA guidance confirms May hike trajectory; CFTC reset allows fresh buyers to engage; oil begins normalising from the geopolitical premium as Iran situation evolves. AUD/CAD grinds from 0.9500 toward 0.9650, testing and eventually breaking the 0.9645 resistance. Phase 3 (Months 4–6): If 0.9700 confirms as a new floor (a break and hold above 2026 highs), CTA and momentum buying add mechanical flows toward 0.9850–1.00. The USMCA review (July 1) is the strategic binary event that defines the upper range — a constructive outcome removes risk premium and could accelerate toward parity; a confrontational outcome caps appreciation at 0.9700. Volatility is expected to decline from the current ~8% annualised (event-premium regime) to approximately 6–7% annualised by April–May, creating a better environment for carry trades. Pullback zones within the strategic horizon: 0.9350–0.9420 (preferred entry), 0.9195 (200d SMA absolute floor).

increase_conditions: RBA hikes to 4.10% AND oil falls below $72 (CAD oil premium fades); AUD/CAD sustains above 0.9700 for 3+ sessions — would increase to 4/5
RBA reaches 4.35%+; BoC April MPR confirms stable/improving outlook; AUD/CAD breaks parity with confirmation; USMCA extension confirmed — would increase to 5/5
decrease_conditions: Iron ore falls below $90/t; China GDP misses at <4.0%; Australia current account widens to -3.5% of GDP — would decrease to 2/5
RBA pauses cycle with explicit signal OR USMCA review begins confrontationally; AUD/CAD closes below 0.9195 (200d SMA) for 3+ days — would decrease to 1/5

risk_reward.expected_value: (50% × +265 pips) + (25% × +490 pips) - (25% × 410 pips) = +132.5 + 122.5 - 102.5 = +152.5 pips positive EV; combined carry + expected capital gain approximately 350 pips total expected return over 5-month strategic horizon
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Modestly positive asymmetry. Base and upside scenarios cover the downside scenario on a probability-weighted basis. The pair's carry accrual (~40 pips per month) adds ~200 pips of positive carry over the 5-month strategic horizon, materially improving the risk/reward relative to a zero-carry pair.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: National Bank of Canada (NBC) cross-implied AUD/CAD approximately 0.9522 (Q1 2026) → 0.9589 (Q2) → 0.9823 (Q3) → 0.9616 (Q4); bank consensus clusters approximately 0.93–0.97 for AUD/CAD by year-end 2026 [Source: NBC Forex Economics and Strategy February 2026]
consensus_divergence.our_tactical_view: Neutral-to-mildly bullish; target 0.9480–0.9540 over 2–4 weeks; prefer entry at 0.9350–0.9420 pullback
consensus_divergence.our_strategic_view: Bullish; target 0.9700–0.9850 by mid-2026; parity (1.00) achievable under upside scenario
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: approximately +300–500 pips above bank consensus year-end forecasts for strategic view
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: Three primary sources of divergence from consensus. First, most consensus AUD/USD forecasts were calibrated when the RBA was cutting (pre-February 2026 hike) — the rerate of AUD from 0.63–0.67 (pre-hike) to 0.70–0.71 (current) has not been fully absorbed into cross-rate forecasts, meaning bank cross-implied AUD/CAD remains stale. Second, bank models use static oil assumptions ($60–70/bbl normalised) but do not adequately price the timing of oil normalisation — the current $81–83 level has given CAD more near-term support than consensus assumed, but this should normalise in H2 2026, improving AUD/CAD more than banks expect. Third, the bilateral investment relationship acceleration (Carney-Albanese MOU, IFM $10bn commitment) is not captured in standard bank FX models, and while it is a modest CAD-positive flow, it does not offset the structural carry advantage that consensus has underpriced following the February RBA hike.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: AUD/CAD reaching 0.9700–0.9850 vs consensus 0.93–0.95 = 200–350 pips outperformance = 2.1–3.7% alpha on the bilateral position
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If AUD/CAD reverts to consensus range (0.93–0.95), the loss from a 0.9510 entry is approximately 60–110 pips (-0.6% to -1.2%) — a modest and bounded loss

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: Tactical conviction 2/5 and strategic conviction 3/5 are divergent in magnitude but directionally consistent (both lean bullish). The divergence reflects transient tactical headwinds — extreme positioning (100th percentile CFTC), resistance at 0.9645, elevated event risk — that have a 4–8 week half-life and do not invalidate the strategic bullish thesis. The framework scores confirm this: tactical net +0.260 vs strategic net +0.513, with the tactical score dampened by positioning and event risk headwinds that normalise by the strategic horizon.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: Late March to mid-April 2026: (a) 17–18 March event resolution removes the super-week binary risk premium; (b) CFTC positioning resets from 100th to 70th–80th percentile (estimated 4–6 week normalisation); (c) 25 March monthly CPI provides the next data point confirming or challenging the RBA's hiking trajectory
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Do not initiate new long AUD/CAD positions at 0.9510 with events imminent and positioning crowded. Use any pullback toward 0.9350–0.9420 as the preferred strategic entry point, where carry at +160 bps, MA support (~0.9439), and improved risk/reward coincide.

forward_vs_spot.forward_1m: ~0.9523 (+13 pips from carry; implies 0.9450–0.9550 expected range)
forward_vs_spot.edge_6m: +110 to +260 pips above forward-implied rate (approximately 1.2–2.7% above forward-implied level)

catalysts.event: Australia February Labour Force Survey + Canadian February LFS
catalysts.rationale: Australia's February LFS is expected to confirm ongoing labour market tightness (~4.1–4.2% unemployment, above consensus beats have been recent trend). Canada's LFS (simultaneous release) is expected to show unemployment ticking higher (RBC consensus). A better-than-expected Australia print alongside a weaker Canadian print would directionally widen the bilateral labour market differential (4.1% vs 6.5%+) and reinforce the RBA's hawkish stance for the 17 March decision. The bilateral divergence in labour market outcomes is the cleanest real-time indicator of the growth differential — specifically relevant for AUD/CAD because the unemployment gap of 240 bps is the widest in a decade and is the primary justification for the policy rate divergence.

Canada's February CPI is expected to spike to ~2.9% headline on oil/gasoline prices. For AUD/CAD, a high Canadian CPI print is ambiguous: it creates a BoC stagflation dilemma (weak growth + rising inflation) that is CAD-negative (BoC cannot cut to support growth) but also reduces the urgency for the bilateral carry trade (tighter BoC would narrow the carry advantage). Core CPI stability (CPI-trim ~2.4–2.5%) is the key watch — any upside surprise in core would meaningfully reprice BoC trajectory. The bilateral transmission is: high Canadian CPI → BoC stagflation dilemma → CAD weakens → AUD/CAD moves higher, but this effect is partial and dominated by the rate channel.

The single most impactful near-term bilateral event. A 38% probability hike to 4.10% would drive AUD/CAD approximately +150–200 pips as it validates the hawkish trajectory and widens the bilateral carry from +160 to +185 bps. Even a hawkish hold ('May is live') would be moderately AUD/CAD positive (+50–80 pips) by repricing May hike probability higher. Only a genuinely dovish hold ('conditions warrant pause') would be AUD/CAD negative, driving the pair toward 0.9350 by triggering CFTC unwind. The consecutive RBA-FOMC setup means AUD/CAD receives two bilateral impulses within 24 hours on March 17–18, making this the most information-dense bilateral catalyst in the near-term horizon.

catalysts.event: FOMC Meeting with SEP/Dot Plot Update + BoC Rate Decision
catalysts.rationale: The FOMC SEP is primarily a USD event but has indirect AUD/CAD implications. A dovish FOMC (2+ cuts in 2026 median dot) would weaken USD broadly, which benefits both AUD and CAD — but benefits AUD more given its higher USD sensitivity. This creates a mild AUD/CAD positive from the FOMC. A hawkish FOMC (0–1 cuts) would strengthen USD and could temporarily compress AUD/CAD by 50–80 pips. The BoC meeting the same day (18 March, hold expected at 94%) compounds the event density but provides minimal new bilateral information — no MPR is released at this meeting. The bilateral impact is dominated by the RBA (prior day) + FOMC combination rather than the BoC alone.

The first major data release after the super-week events and the 'verdict' on the RBA's hiking trajectory. February CPI consensus is approximately 3.6–3.7% trimmed mean. A hot print at or above 3.8% (matching January's 3.4%) would re-price May hike probability toward 70%+ and be materially AUD/CAD positive (+80–120 pips). A cooling print below 3.3% would defer May hike expectations and provide a tactical relief rally for AUD/CAD shorts (-60–100 pips). This release has asymmetric AUD/CAD implications because Canada has no simultaneous high-importance data release — it is a pure AUD driver with direct bilateral transmission through the rate channel.

China PMIs are an AUD-specific driver with minimal CAD impact, making them an asymmetric pair catalyst. NBS Manufacturing PMI in contraction (49.0 February) represents an AUD headwind through the commodity demand channel. Any improvement above 50 would be disproportionately AUD/CAD positive. Conversely, further deterioration toward 47–48 would renew iron ore demand concerns and disproportionately weaken AUD vs CAD — the most direct expression of the 'AUD is China-sensitive, CAD is US-sensitive' bilateral differentiation. AUD has 30–35% China export exposure while Canada's China exposure is minimal, making China PMI a highly asymmetric bilateral catalyst.

catalysts.event: RBA Rate Decision + Quarterly Statement on Monetary Policy (SoMP)
catalysts.rationale: The May SoMP contains updated CPI and GDP forecasts and is the most important bilateral event in the strategic horizon. If trimmed mean CPI forecasts are revised higher from the current 3.7% mid-2026 projection, the terminal rate repricing extends — a direct AUD/CAD catalyst. The market prices a full 25 bps hike to 4.10% at May. The SoMP will confirm or revise this expectation. A hawkish SoMP (4.35% terminal) would extend the carry advantage to +210 bps over BoC and drive AUD/CAD above 0.9700. Simultaneously, the BoC's April 29 MPR (same date) provides the bilateral context — if BoC April MPR shows upward GDP revision, the divergence narrows modestly; if GDP revised down, divergence widens further.

If OPEC+ accelerates production increases beyond the current +206K bbl/d pace, WTI falls toward $65–70. This removes the oil-driven CAD premium that has supported CAD during the Iran episode and allows AUD/CAD's rate differential advantage to express more cleanly. Oil normalisation is net bullish AUD/CAD by removing CAD's oil tailwind — the bilateral transmission is: OPEC+ production increase → WTI falls → CAD terms-of-trade deteriorates → AUD/CAD rises as the primary commodity headwind for the pair fades. The magnitude is approximately +80–150 pips if WTI falls from $82 to $68 (a full normalisation toward consensus estimates).

The USMCA review is a CAD-specific risk event with negligible direct AUD impact. A constructive USMCA review would reduce the structural risk premium in USD/CAD, strengthening CAD and creating mild AUD/CAD headwinds (as CAD strengthens against all pairs). A confrontational review would weaken CAD broadly, creating AUD/CAD upside from the CAD-weakness channel. For AUD/CAD specifically, the bilateral impact is secondary to the USD/CAD move, but positions of roughly 50–100 pips in either direction are plausible depending on the July 1 tone. The USMCA outcome is the strategic binary event that defines the upper range — a constructive outcome removes risk premium and could accelerate AUD/CAD toward parity.

invalidators.condition: Australia Q2 CPI trimmed mean falls below 3.0% YoY
invalidators.mechanism: The RBA's primary rationale for tightening would be undermined, collapsing the 160 bps bilateral carry advantage that underpins the pair. The pair could rapidly de-rate from 0.9510 toward 0.9200–0.9300 as market reprices RBA to hold rather than hike, removing the structural justification for elevated AUD/CAD above PPP fair value of 0.91.

invalidators.condition: WTI crude oil sustains above $100/bbl for 2+ consecutive months post-Iran resolution
invalidators.mechanism: Sustained oil strength would maintain CAD's terms-of-trade premium beyond the expected transient window, while simultaneously increasing global inflation risk that reduces growth expectations for commodity importers (including Australia's iron ore customers). The net bilateral effect would be approximately flat for AUD/CAD fundamentals but technically reversal risk from AUD crowding would grow as CAD's commodity tailwind persists unexpectedly.

invalidators.condition: CFTC AUD net longs rise above +80,000 contracts while CAD leveraged fund shorts also increase above -60,000 contracts simultaneously
invalidators.mechanism: A dual extreme positioning event of this magnitude has historically preceded bilateral pair corrections of 5–8% within 4–6 weeks as both legs unwind simultaneously. The pair-level crowding risk would become systemic rather than merely elevated, creating a mechanical reversal dynamic where the AUD/CAD long trade becomes self-defeating.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Weekly CFTC COT Traders in Financial Futures report

invalidators.condition: Iron ore 62% Fe falls below $85/t sustained for 3+ consecutive weeks AND WCS-WTI spread widens above $18/bbl simultaneously
invalidators.mechanism: Australia's primary export commodity weakens while Canada's crude oil export premium expands — the net commodity differential shifts from mildly AUD-positive to materially CAD-positive, creating a double-negative for AUD/CAD's fundamental underpinning. This simultaneous adverse move in both commodity legs would eliminate the 'moderate bullish' commodity thesis that currently provides +0.114 strategic contribution.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: SGX iron ore futures + Canadian crude differential data (CanOils/NEB)

invalidators.condition: Australian current account deficit widens to -4.0% of GDP or beyond in Q1 2026 data (reported June)
invalidators.mechanism: The structural AUD overvaluation argument gains traction — a -4.0% CA deficit at AUD/CAD near 0.95 is inconsistent with external balance sustainability, and the market would begin pricing mean-reversion toward OECD PPP fair value of 0.91. The current account deterioration would offset the carry premium and raise questions about AUD's fundamental support at levels above 0.93.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: ABS Balance of Payments quarterly release

signposts.confirms_if: RBA terminal reprices above 4.25% (May meeting) AND BoC remains on hold — bilateral differential widening to +185+ bps
signposts.invalidates_if: RBA terminal falls below 3.85% (hold then cut priced) OR BoC begins pricing cuts below 2.25%
signposts.bilateral_importance: This is the primary driver for AUD/CAD specifically — the differential between Australia's hiking cycle and Canada's neutral-lower-bound position is more impactful for this pair than either currency individually. No other G10 pair has a comparable asymmetry between an actively hiking central bank and one at the lower bound of neutral.

signposts.confirms_if: Oil falls toward $65–70 (WTI/iron ore ratio narrows to ~0.65) — removes CAD oil premium; AUD/CAD commodity net becomes unambiguously positive
signposts.invalidates_if: Oil remains above $90 sustained AND iron ore falls below $90 — simultaneous commodity divergence that is maximally bearish for AUD/CAD
signposts.bilateral_importance: This ratio captures the net commodity terms-of-trade differential for AUD/CAD in a single indicator — far more relevant for this pair than tracking either commodity independently. AUD/CAD is the purest expression of the iron ore vs oil commodity rivalry in G10 FX.

signposts.indicator: CFTC Bilateral Positioning (AUD Net Longs + CAD Net Shorts)
signposts.current_level: AUD net +67,762 contracts (100th percentile); CAD leveraged fund shorts -41,371; combined bilateral ~$9.6 billion notional
signposts.confirms_if: AUD CFTC resets to 65th–75th percentile AND CAD leveraged fund shorts reduce to -15,000 to -25,000 (healthy rather than crowded level)
signposts.invalidates_if: AUD CFTC rises above +80,000 contracts AND CAD shorts rise above -60,000 simultaneously
signposts.bilateral_importance: AUD/CAD is explicitly the pair most directly exposed to this dual-positioning dynamic — CFTC data for each individual currency must be combined for this pair's position crowding assessment, which is not done in either standalone dossier. The bilateral positioning at $9.6 billion notional is a pair-specific risk not visible in either single-currency CFTC report.

signposts.confirms_if: Spot holds above 0.9439 50d SMA through tactical pullback AND MA continues rising above 0.9500 within 6 weeks
signposts.invalidates_if: Spot closes below 0.9439 for 3+ consecutive sessions (short-term trend break) — especially concerning given the 100-day MA (~0.9200–0.9300) is the next major technical support
signposts.bilateral_importance: AUD/CAD's technical structure specifically (not the individual currency MAs from either dossier) determines whether CTA flows provide a tailwind or headwind. The 50d SMA at 0.9439 is the key near-term technical support for this pair and the level at which the preferred strategic entry zone begins — making it both a technical and fundamental signpost simultaneously.

signposts.indicator: Australia-Canada GDP Growth Gap (Monthly Proxy: PMI Differential)
signposts.current_level: Australia Composite PMI 52.4 vs Canada Services PMI 46.5 — differential: +5.9 ppts in Australia's favour
signposts.confirms_if: Differential sustains above +4 ppts (Australia expansion, Canada near-contraction) — validates RBA hawkishness vs BoC neutral stance and maintains the growth channel contribution to bilateral carry divergence
signposts.invalidates_if: Differential narrows below +1 ppt (Canadian PMI recovery to 50+; Australian PMI deceleration below 51) — would signal growth convergence that undermines the policy divergence justification for the pair
signposts.bilateral_importance: The PMI differential is the highest-frequency proxy for the growth divergence that underpins the rate differential — specifically relevant for AUD/CAD because both economies are similar in size and structure, making the PMI gap a clean bilateral indicator not distorted by structural differences (unlike comparing Australia to Japan or Switzerland).
signposts.source: Judo Bank Australia Composite PMI (S&P Global); Canadian S&P Global Services PMI (https://www.spglobal.com/marketintelligence/en/mi/research-analysis/global-pmi.html)

assumptions: Spot reconciliation: Two dossiers show slightly different AUD/CAD levels (0.952 in AUD dossier vs 0.959 in CAD dossier). This likely reflects different fixing times on the same day. The analysis uses the midpoint of approximately 0.9510–0.9520 as the reference level, consistent with the Yahoo Finance live rate of 0.9510 as of March 11 at 23:37 GMT.

Forward points calculation: AUD/CAD forward points estimated using interest rate parity (carry of +160 bps annualised). Actual forward points will incorporate the cross-currency basis for AUD/CAD (itself derived from AUD/USD basis and CAD/USD basis), which may differ by ±5–10 pips. The xccy basis net for AUD/CAD is estimated at approximately +15–25 bps (slightly larger forward premium to AUD than pure IRP suggests), but this is not material to the analysis.

Weight adjustments: Three factors adjusted (Factor 1 +0.03 tactical/strategic, Factor 5 +0.03 strategic, Factors 3 and 8 reduced to offset). All adjustments are documented and consistent with the framework rules (maximum ±0.05 per factor; sum = 1.00). Tactical and strategic weights are independently verified to sum to 1.00.

Commodity net exposure: The netting of AUD vs CAD commodity sensitivities uses the framework directional rules. The analysis acknowledges that the AUD-commodities correlation, which used to hover around 0.7–0.8, has fallen closer to 0.4–0.5 in recent quarters, meaning commodity transmission is less reliable than historically. This is reflected in moderate rather than high confidence levels for Factor 5.

Bilateral investment flows: The Carney-Albanese MOU (March 5, 2026) creates a new structural bilateral flow that is not captured in standard CFTC or forward markets data. The estimated IFM $10 billion Canadian investment commitment by Australian super funds represents approximately $2–3 billion per year of AUD-selling/CAD-buying — a modest but non-zero structural headwind to AUD/CAD appreciation. This partially offsets the carry advantage and is reflected in the Conviction 3/5 rather than 4/5 strategic assessment.

AUD/CAD 1M IV of approximately 7.5–8.5% is estimated from the combination of AUD/USD 1M IV (~11.5% from the AUD dossier) and USD/CAD IV structure (~6.5%), as direct AUD/CAD option data was not available. The estimate may differ from actual market pricing by ±1–1.5 vols.

CAD CFTC percentile estimate of 70th–75th for leveraged fund shorts is derived from the reported -41,371 contract position against historical ranges; the precise percentile is uncertain given incomplete historical data in the dossiers.
